title: Next and Break 🛑
description: Learn how to skip ahead or completely escape a loop.

slides.title: Controlling the Flow 🏄‍♂️
slides.content:
Once a loop or iterator starts, does it HAVE to finish? 

No!

Ruby gives you two keywords to hijack the flow of a loop from the inside: `next` and `break`.

slides.title: The Next Keyword ⏭️
slides.content:
If you want to completely skip the current cycle of the loop and jump straight to the exact next iteration, you use `next`.

```ruby
(1..5).each do |num|
  next if num == 3
  puts num
end
```
It prints: `1 2 4 5`. It totally skipped over `3`!

slides.title: Skipping Evens
slides.content: Run this! Notice how the `next` keyword jumps past any even number.
slides.codeSnippet:
(1..5).each do |i|
  next if i.even?
  puts i
end

quizData.question: What happens when Ruby hits the `next` keyword?

options.text: It stops the current iteration entirely and jumps directly to the start of the next one.
It skips back to the previous iteration.

quizData.explanation: `next` says "I'm done with this one, give me the next item immediately."

slides.title: The Break Keyword 🛑
slides.content:
If you don't want to skip, but instead vastly want to escape and **destroy the entire loop**, you use the `break` keyword.

It throws you immediately outside the loop, causing it to terminate permanently.

slides.title: Pulling the Emergency Brake
slides.content: Even though this loop is supposed to run 10 times, the `break` keyword destroys it after just 3 iterations.
slides.codeSnippet:
(1..10).each do |i|
  if i == 4
    break
  end
  puts i
end

puts "Loop destroyed!"

quizData.question: Which keyword permanently aborts the ongoing loop structure?
quizData.explanation: `break` throws you entirely out of the loop. `next` only skips the current turn.
